one_line_takeaway: DFSU shows that inversion-derived pseudo-PII surrogates, combined with a LoRA-constrained contrastive loss that localizes forgetting to entity tokens, can approach oracle-level (real-data) unlearning performance on Pythia models up to 1.4B without ever touching the original training data.
key_finding: On Scenario I (WikiText+PII, generative), DFSU reduces ERR to 0.00% at all three scales, matching the Oracle's 0.00% on the strictest exact-match leakage criterion, starting from original-model ERR of 0.80% (160M), 20.40% (410M), and 21.40% (1.4B). On the softer FRS metric, DFSU stays close to Oracle: e.g. Pythia-410M FRS is 3.88% (DFSU) vs 3.46% (Oracle), with WikiText PPL only rising from 8.69 (Oracle) to 8.83 (DFSU). On Scenario II (MNLI+PII, reasoning), original-model S-Exp is as high as 50.20% for Pythia-1.4B; DFSU drives this down to 1.20% while MNLI accuracy is 77.05% (DFSU) vs 77.21% (Oracle) at 1.4B, and 68.45% (DFSU) vs 69.90% (Oracle) at 410M. The ablation shows PSCU outperforms full-sequence GA: pushing GA to match PSCU's near-zero E-Hit leakage on Pythia-410M (~0.13%) causes WikiText perplexity to explode beyond 4x10^4, whereas PSCU holds PPL at 8.83; on MNLI, GA can reach E-Hit 0.0% but only by crashing accuracy to 57.35%, while PSCU achieves comparable E-Hit (0.38%) at 68.45% accuracy. A LoRA-module ablation (Table 3) finds privacy removal is largely architecture-agnostic (E-Hit < 1.6% across MLP-only/Attention-only/Full configurations) but utility preservation is module- and task-dependent: MLP-only is best for generation (Full increases 410M PPL from 8.83 to 10.23), while Attention-based adaptation is competitive for MNLI (highest 410M accuracy 69.9%). A data-scale ablation (Fig. 5) shows privacy leakage reduction saturates by ~100 pseudo-samples (matching the 500-sample baseline), while utility retention continues to improve with more pseudo-data (WikiText ~5.4% decay at scale 100 vs MNLI ~12.1% decay at scale 100 dropping to ~2.8% at full scale). A hyperparameter sweep over privacy weight beta and LoRA rank/alpha identifies beta≈1.0 with r=4, alpha>=32 as the recommended stable regime, reaching E-Hit < 1% by epoch 6 with under 6% PPL increase.
core_contribution: The paper proposes DFSU (Data-Free Selective Unlearning), a three-stage framework for removing memorized PII from a pretrained LLM's weights without any access to the original training data. Stage 1 trains a sequence-to-sequence inversion model (Flan-T5-Large) to reconstruct text from a target model's output log-probability distribution. Stage 2 queries the target model with entity-swapped candidate prompts (built from reused PII syntactic templates with substitute entities), decodes the resulting logits via the trained inverter into pseudo-PII text, and annotates sensitive spans in that pseudo-text via few-shot LLM prompting, yielding an annotated surrogate dataset Dpseudo. Stage 3 (Privacy-Selective Contrastive Unlearning, PSCU) freezes the base model and trains only a LoRA adapter (applied to MLP modules by default, rank r=4, alpha=32) with a dual-stream contrastive loss J(phi) = alpha*L_gen - beta*L_priv, which maximizes loss on masked privacy tokens (L_priv) while minimizing loss on the surrounding non-sensitive context tokens (L_gen), so forgetting is localized to entity tokens rather than applied across full sequences.
threat_model: Defensive/remediation setting, not an attacker threat model: a defender (model owner) holds only the deployed model's weights (white-box access to its logits) and knows the general category of PII to be forgotten (e.g., IP addresses, device identifiers), but does not have access to the original training corpus or the specific forget set S, because it is proprietary, subject to 'right to be forgotten' legal requirements, or unrecoverable at scale. The paper explicitly repurposes model-inversion attacks (normally an adversarial extraction technique, e.g. Vec2Text, logit-based inversion) as a defensive tool to synthesize a surrogate forget set. Stated limitation: DFSU requires white-box access to model logits and is not applicable in black-box deployment settings.
limitations: Self-reported: DFSU requires white-box access to the target model's output logits, which is a barrier in black-box deployment settings (the paper's only explicitly stated limitation). Additional limitations evident from the paper's own text: the inversion model is trained only once on Pythia-410M and reused across scales by assuming shared architecture, so generalization to architecturally different model families is untested; pseudo-PII synthesis depends on reusing the syntactic templates of the original PII data with entity-swapped substitutes, i.e., it presumes the defender already knows the structural format of the sensitive data even without the instances themselves; the In-the-Wild evaluation (Sec 5.2) is qualitative (representative examples only, no aggregate leakage metric reported) and the paper itself characterizes the observed effect as a 'shift in the conditional distribution over PII-like entity realizations, rather than a narrow removal of a single memorized verbatim suffix' -- i.e., entities are substituted with other plausible-looking values rather than confirmed as fully non-recoverable.

datasets_used: AI4Privacy PII-Masking-200k (source of sensitive PII injected into corpora; 500 unique PII samples used, partitioned into 10 groups of 50)
WikiText-103 (general corpus for the generative/Scenario I task)
MNLI (general corpus for the classification/reasoning Scenario II task)
quantitative_results: Scenario I (WikiText+PII) ERR: Original Model 0.80% (160M) / 20.40% (410M) / 21.40% (1.4B); Oracle 0.00% at all scales; DFSU 0.00% at all scales
Scenario I Pythia-410M FRS: Oracle 3.46% vs DFSU 3.88%; PPL: Oracle 8.69 vs DFSU 8.83
Scenario I Pythia-1.4B: Oracle FRS 5.83%/PPL 7.13 vs DFSU FRS 4.42%/PPL 7.23
Scenario II (MNLI+PII) S-Exp: Original Model 50.20% (1.4B) vs DFSU 1.20%
Scenario II Pythia-1.4B accuracy: Oracle 77.21% vs DFSU 77.05%; Pythia-410M accuracy: Oracle 69.90% vs DFSU 68.45%; Pythia-160M accuracy: Oracle 44.06% vs DFSU 43.38%
GA ablation on Pythia-410M/WikiText: GA at near-zero leakage causes PPL to exceed 4x10^4 vs PSCU stable PPL 8.83
GA ablation on MNLI/Pythia-410M: GA reaches E-Hit 0.0% but accuracy drops to 57.35%, vs PSCU E-Hit 0.38% at accuracy 68.45%
LoRA target-module ablation (Table 3), Pythia-410M WikiText: MLP E-Hit 0.13%/PPL 8.83; Attention E-Hit 0.00%/PPL 9.98; Full E-Hit 0.00%/PPL 10.23
LoRA target-module ablation, Pythia-410M MNLI: MLP E-Hit 0.38%/Acc 68.5%; Attention E-Hit 0.31%/Acc 69.9%; Full E-Hit 0.13%/Acc 67.0%
Data-scale ablation: leakage reduction saturates near 100 pseudo-samples; WikiText utility decay ~5.4% at scale 100; MNLI decay ~12.1% at scale 100 vs ~2.8% at full 500-sample scale
Inversion model quality gate stated in methodology: F1 > 30%, BLEU > 15% required for usable pseudo-labels
Hyperparameter recommendation: beta≈1.0, LoRA r=4, alpha>=32 reaches E-Hit < 1% by epoch 6 with < 6% PPL increase
baselines_compared: Original Model (no unlearning, i.e. the memorized/leaky checkpoint before any intervention)
Original Data (Oracle): identical PSCU unlearning procedure but using real ground-truth PII samples instead of inversion-derived pseudo-PII -- upper-bound comparator
Full-Sequence Gradient Ascent (GA) under the same LoRA budget and hyperparameters, used as an ablation baseline to isolate the effect of PSCU's token-selective loss
